message.content: Adversaries may exploit the Microsoft Office "Office Test" Registry key to maintain persistence on compromised systems. This key allows the specification of an arbitrary DLL to be executed each time an Office application, like Word or Excel, is launched. Typically used by Microsoft for testing and debugging, this Registry key is not created by default during Office installation. Both user-specific and global Registry keys exist for this feature. Attackers, such as APT28, have been known to add this key and point it to a malicious DLL, ensuring their code runs whenever an Office application starts. The potential impact includes unauthorized code execution and persistent access to the system. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and auditing Registry changes, particularly for the Office Test keys, and employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized DLLs from executing.
